### Post by mitch2k2 on 2008-04-13
Hey all,

After trying like mad to set the video driver (it's an XGI Volari XP5) and not being able to make anything work, including desktop effects, I pulled up my xorg.conf file and it looks like this:
```
# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Generic Keyboard"
	Driver		"kbd"
	Option		"XkbRules"	"xorg"
	Option		"XkbModel"	"pc105"
	Option		"XkbLayout"	"us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Configured Mouse"
	Driver		"vmmouse"
	Option		"CorePointer"
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"Configured Video Device"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"Configured Monitor"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Monitor		"Configured Monitor"
	Device		"Configured Video Device"
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
	Screen		"Default Screen"
EndSection
```Strange, no? Like nothing's configured. I've run reconfigure several times now. And, when I've gone to Screen and Graphics to set the monitor (Dell 770s) that produces a thoroughly janked screen.

And then, I'm having strange issues with the kernel, too. On my laptop, Hardy's upgraded to the .16 kernel, but on this buggy desktop install, it's still  at 12. 

Any ideas?

# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using

    Identifier    "Generic Keyboard"

### Post by Tuxor on 2008-04-13
I'm in the same kind of trouble. I have a laptop "Clevo M720R" with an Intel graphics card in it. When I dock it, it uses the docks (Targus ACP60EU) graphics card, "XGI Volari Z7"  in stead. Why I don't know, but it makes the laptop run cooler and less noise from the fan, so that's good. However, I can't get the correct resolution on the screen I use with the dock. It's a Samsung SyncMaster 940MW with 1440x900 as native resolution. I can only get it to run in 1280x1024 smoothly. I've been able to force it in to 1440x900 by manually editing xorg.conf modes part, but then the screen locks up for a couple of seconds every time I open a new window or scroll. My xorg looks just like yours. I've also tried forcing the "sis" driver with the same result.

Seems to me that were out in the cold using XGI cards.

### Post by Tuxor on 2008-04-20
Ok, found out how to fix this myself on Ubuntu 8.04.

First:
This worked for me, every installation and hardware setup differs. Handle this information with care, and make sure to make backups of every file before modifying it. If you don't know what you're doing, or if you cant risk to loose data, then don't experiment!
That said.

About the video driver:

- The "sis" driver works. So I backed up the /etc/X11/xorg.conf file to have the original. Then I did some modifications to the /etc/X11/xorg.conf file. Posting it below to show what lines I added.

(Make sure you have the sis package installed so the system can make use of that driver. Install it with: sudo apt-get install xserver-xorg-video-sis from a terminal.)

- This xorg.conf worked excellent for the "XGI Volari Z7" videocard and the "Samsung SyncMaster 940MW" screen using 1440x900 resolution. (But it doesn't work with desktop effects.)
```

# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier    "Generic Keyboard"
    Driver        "kbd"
    Option        "XkbRules"    "xorg"
    Option        "XkbModel"    "pc105"
    Option        "XkbLayout"    "se"
    Option        "XkbOptions"    "lv3:ralt_switch"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier    "Configured Mouse"
    Driver        "mouse"
    Option        "CorePointer"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier    "Synaptics Touchpad"
    Driver        "synaptics"
    Option        "SendCoreEvents"    "true"
    Option        "Device"        "/dev/psaux"
    Option        "Protocol"        "auto-dev"
    Option        "HorizEdgeScroll"    "0"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier    "Configured Video Device"
    **Driver        "sis"**
#    BusID        "0000:05:02.0"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier    "Default Screen"
    Monitor        "Configured Monitor"
    Device        "Configured Video Device"
        [B]SubSection    "Display"
            Modes    "1400x1050" "1440x900" "1280x1024" "1024x768"
        EndSubSection[/B]
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier    "Default Layout"
    Screen        "Default Screen"
    InputDevice    "Synaptics Touchpad"
EndSection
```The next issue was to boot with separate xorg.conf files, one when the laptop was in the dock using the docks "XGI video card", and one when the laptop starts without the dock using its internal videocard. I solved this by installing a application called "switchconf". It's available through the synaptic package manager, at least in 8.04.

I tought the documentation was a little short on switchconf, so hopefully I can help someone by describing how I did it a little more detailed:

1. I installed the switchconf with synaptic package manager.
   This created a new folder /etc/switchconf containing a "conf" file for the switchconf application and two subfolders.
   The conf file is used if you want to change working paths and some other application parameters for switchconf. No need to change those parameters.
What more happens, is that a script called S37switchconf is created in /etc/rcS.d folder. This script is run on every startup of Ubuntu. It parses the GRUB bootloader startup command and looks for a parameter called switchconf. This means that by adding a switchconf parameter to the initstring on startup you can tell switchconf which profile it should load. (More on that under number 5.)

2. I created new folders in (under) /dev/switchconf, one for each startup profile I wanted to use. You decide yourself what you want to call them. Since I wanted one profile when starting docked, and another when starting portable, I chose to call them "docked" and "portable".
(I created the folders from the terminal with sudo mkdir [folder name], since the folders is root level.)
Now the structure looked like this.

[IMG]http://procudo.se/1.gif[/IMG]

3. What switchconf does is to load different "conf" files in to folders. You tell switchconf where to load the files by creating a similar folder/file structure in each of the profile folders (in my case the "docked" and "portable" folders). Since the variable file for me is the /etc/X11/xorg.conf, I had to create more folders under each profile folder so it looked like this. 

[IMG]http://procudo.se/2.gif[/IMG]

4. Next step was to put the right file in each of the X11 folders. I put the xorg.conf file that works when I'm docked in the /etc/switchconf/docked/etc/X11 folder and the original in /etc/switchconf/portable/etc/X11.
Now you can start to use the switchconf if you want. From terminal try "sudo switchconf docked" which loads the /etc/switchconf/docked/etc/X11/xorg.conf file in to /etc/X11. It does this by adding a softlink. In this case, since I altered the xorg.conf, I didn't see any changes until I restarted the xserver. You can do this by ctrl-alt-backspace command, make sure you have saved what youre doing before this, because it pops right out of your session.

5. Ok, so now switchconf could alter the xorg.conf file from terminal. Now I extended it so I could choose what xorg.conf (switchconf profile) I wanted to use from the GRUB boot menu. Open up your /boot/grub/menu.lst for editing with sudo gedit /boot/grub/menu.lst from terminal.
I duplicated the first (default) boot entry and added the switchconf command to booth of those entries, telling what switchconf profile it would use.
My menu.lst now looks like this.
```

more stuff...

## ## End Default Options ##

title        Ubuntu 8.04, kernel 2.6.24-16-generic **(PORTABLE)**
root        (hd0,0)
kernel        /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.24-16-generic root=UUID=zzzzz-zzzz-zzzz-zzzz-zzzzzzzzzz ro **switchconf=portable** quiet splash
initrd        /boot/initrd.img-2.6.24-16-generic
quiet

title        Ubuntu 8.04, kernel 2.6.24-16-generic **(DOCKED)**
root        (hd0,0)
kernel        /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.24-16-generic root=UUID=zzzzz-zzzz-zzzz-zzzz-zzzzzzzzzz ro **switchconf=docked** quiet splash
initrd        /boot/initrd.img-2.6.24-16-generic
quiet

title        Ubuntu 8.04, kernel 2.6.24-16-generic (recovery mode)
root        (hd0,0)
kernel        /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.24-16-generic root=UUID=zzzzz-zzzz-zzzz-zzzz-zzzzzzzzzz ro single
initrd        /boot/initrd.img-2.6.24-16-generic

...more stuff
```This way I can choose on startup which xorg.conf (switchconf profile) I want to use.

Next step will be to combine this with with the "whereami" program so that the right switchconf profile will be used based on what hardware is present. Haven't done that yet. But then again, I like to have some control over boot and other options. That's part of why I once ran away from M$ :) So maybe I leave it with manually choosing from the GRUB menu.

---
